title: Quiz: Quantum Molecular Dynamics
description: Test your understanding of ab initio molecular dynamics

questions.question: What is the key difference between classical MD and ab initio MD?

options.text: AIMD computes forces from quantum mechanics rather than force fields
AIMD uses classical mechanics for electrons
AIMD is faster than classical MD

questions.explanation: In ab initio MD, forces are computed from quantum mechanical electronic structure calculations (like DFT) at each time step, rather than from parameterized classical force fields.

questions.question: What does the Born-Oppenheimer approximation assume?

options.text: Electrons instantaneously adjust to nuclear positions
Electronic and nuclear motion are coupled
All atoms have the same mass

questions.explanation: The Born-Oppenheimer approximation assumes that electrons are much faster than nuclei, so they instantaneously adjust to any nuclear configuration. This allows solving for electronic structure at each fixed nuclear geometry.

questions.question: What is unique about Car-Parrinello molecular dynamics?

options.text: It uses larger time steps than BOMD
It treats electronic orbitals as dynamical variables with fictitious mass
It only works for classical force fields

questions.explanation: Car-Parrinello MD introduces a fictitious mass μ for electronic orbitals and propagates them dynamically alongside nuclei. This avoids full SCF convergence at each step.

questions.question: What are Pulay forces?

options.text: Additional force terms arising from basis functions that depend on nuclear positions

questions.explanation: Pulay forces arise when using atom-centered basis sets (like Gaussians) that move with the nuclei. The basis set dependence on R adds extra terms beyond the Hellmann-Feynman force.

questions.question: Plane wave basis sets have no Pulay force contributions because they don't depend on nuclear positions.
questions.explanation: True. Plane waves are defined in reciprocal space and don't move with atoms, so there are no basis set derivative terms. The Hellmann-Feynman theorem gives exact forces.

questions.question: When is non-adiabatic dynamics necessary?

options.text: When electronic states are nearly degenerate or crossing
When using plane wave basis sets

questions.explanation: Non-adiabatic dynamics is needed when electronic states approach each other (conical intersections) or cross. The Born-Oppenheimer approximation breaks down in these regions.

questions.question: What is the typical scaling of DFT-based AIMD?
questions.explanation: DFT scales as O(N³) due to matrix diagonalization or related operations. This is applied at each MD step, making DFT-AIMD computationally demanding but still tractable for hundreds of atoms.

questions.question: Which are advantages of Car-Parrinello over Born-Oppenheimer MD? (Select all)

options.text: No full SCF at each step
Works with any electronic structure method

questions.explanation: CPMD avoids full SCF convergence at each step by propagating orbitals dynamically, and provides very stable dynamics. However, it requires smaller time steps than BOMD and works best with plane-wave DFT.
